fields_to_author.informational_note: Iodine affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

drafts._source_mechanism: Reishi (Ganoderma lucidum) beta-glucans stimulate immune responses including macrophage activation, NK cell enhancement, and increased immunoglobulin production. In autoimmune conditions, this immunostimulation may amplify the pathological immune response.
drafts._source_action: Use with caution in autoimmune conditions. Discuss with a physician before use if on immunosuppressive therapy.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Reishi has mild immune-modulating activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your specialist before adding reishi.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Reishi affects immune function — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Vitamin D exerts broad immunomodulatory effects, generally shifting immune responses toward tolerance via Treg cell upregulation and Th17 suppression. Low vitamin D status is associated with increased autoimmune risk. Supplementation may be beneficial in Hashimoto thyroiditis and multiple sclerosis, but high doses in active autoimmune disease require monitoring.
drafts._source_action: Vitamin D supplementation at standard doses (1000–4000 IU/day) is generally safe in autoimmune conditions. High-dose vitamin D requires physician-monitored 25-OH vitamin D levels to avoid toxicity.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild immune-modulating activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your specialist before adding Vitamin D.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Vitamin D affects immune function — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Andrographis paniculata exhibits immunostimulatory activity, upregulating T-cell proliferation, NK cell activity, and cytokine production (IL-2, IFN-γ). In patients with autoimmune conditions relying on immune suppression for disease control, stimulation of autoreactive immune pathways could theoretically exacerbate disease activity.
drafts._source_action: Use caution in patients with autoimmune conditions (lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, IBD). Consult treating physician before use. Monitor for symptom flares.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Andrographis has mild immune-modulating activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your specialist before adding andrographis.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Andrographis affects immune function — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Cordyceps (Cordyceps sinensis/militaris) contains cordycepin (3-deoxyadenosine) and beta-glucans that stimulate NK cell activity, macrophage activation, and T-helper cell proliferation via Toll-like receptor and NFkappaB pathways. This immune potentiation may exacerbate autoimmune conditions (e.g., rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, multiple sclerosis, inflammatory bowel disease).
drafts._source_action: Avoid unsupervised use in autoimmune conditions. Consult a rheumatologist or immunologist before starting cordyceps.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Cordyceps has mild immune-modulating activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your specialist before adding cordyceps.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Cordyceps affects immune function — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: In severely immunocompromised patients (solid organ transplant recipients, hematologic malignancy, high-dose corticosteroid therapy), orally administered probiotic organisms — particularly Lactobacillus spp. and Saccharomyces boulardii — have been documented in case reports to translocate across disrupted mucosal barriers, causing bacteremia or fungemia. Risk is low in healthy individuals but clinically meaningful in profound immunosuppression.
drafts._source_action: Use caution in severely immunocompromised patients (transplant recipients, patients on high-dose immunosuppression). Discuss risk-benefit with transplant team or treating physician before initiating probiotic supplements. Risk does not apply to mild immunosuppression (e.g., low-dose prednisone).

fields_to_author.alert_body: Probiotics has mild immune-modulating activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your specialist before adding probiotics.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Probiotics affects immune function — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Rhodiola rosea exerts immunomodulatory effects including differential modulation of T-regulatory cell differentiation, IFN-gamma production, and NK-cell activity in vitro and in vivo. Immune stimulation may worsen autoimmune disease activity or counteract immunosuppressive therapy.
drafts._source_action: Avoid in patients with active autoimmune disease (lupus, MS, rheumatoid arthritis) or those on immunosuppressive therapy. Consult prescribing physician before use.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Rhodiola has mild immune-modulating activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your specialist before adding rhodiola.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Rhodiola affects immune function — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: CBD inhibits CYP3A4, which metabolizes tacrolimus and sirolimus. FDA drug labels for both tacrolimus and sirolimus explicitly warn that CBD co-administration increases their plasma concentrations, risking nephrotoxicity and immunosuppressive toxicity.
drafts._source_action: Monitor tacrolimus/sirolimus trough levels closely. Dose reduction for the immunosuppressant should be considered. Inform transplant team of CBD use.

fields_to_author.alert_body: CBD has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding CBD.
fields_to_author.informational_note: CBD affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

drafts._source_mechanism: CYP3A4 inhibition by schisandra lignans clinically increased tacrolimus AUC 2.4-fold and midazolam AUC 2.1-fold. Bosutinib AUC is predicted to increase ~3-fold by PBPK modelling. Immunosuppressants with narrow therapeutic indices (tacrolimus, cyclosporine, sirolimus) are predominantly CYP3A4 substrates — schisandra co-administration risks toxic drug accumulation and organ damage.
drafts._source_action: Contraindicated with calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine) and mTOR inhibitors without therapeutic drug monitoring. If clinically necessary, initiate TDM immediately and titrate immunosuppressant dose downward.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Schisandra has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding schisandra.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Schisandra affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

drafts._source_mechanism: Reishi beta-glucan immune stimulation may oppose immunosuppressant pharmacological effects. Effect is likely milder than Echinacea or Astragalus but warrants monitoring.
drafts._source_action: Avoid concurrent use with immunosuppressants without physician oversight, particularly in transplant patients.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Reishi has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding reishi.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Reishi affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

drafts._source_mechanism: Andrographis's immunostimulatory effects may pharmacodynamically oppose immunosuppressant medications (calcineurin inhibitors, corticosteroids, biologics), potentially reducing their therapeutic efficacy or triggering rejection episodes in transplant recipients.
drafts._source_action: Avoid use in transplant recipients or patients on immunosuppressive therapy for serious autoimmune conditions without specialist guidance.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Andrographis has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding andrographis.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Andrographis affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

drafts._source_mechanism: Immunosuppressant drugs (calcineurin inhibitors, mTOR inhibitors, high-dose corticosteroids, biologic agents) impair mucosal and systemic immune defenses, increasing the risk that probiotic microorganisms may cause opportunistic infection. Lactobacillus bacteremia and Saccharomyces fungemia have been documented in patients on immunosuppressive regimens, particularly in transplant and oncology settings.
drafts._source_action: In patients on immunosuppressive therapy for transplant or oncologic indications, consult with the prescribing team before adding probiotic supplements. For patients on mild immunosuppression (e.g., methotrexate for RA at low doses), risk is substantially lower and probiotics may be appropriate.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Probiotics has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding probiotics.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Probiotics affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

drafts._source_mechanism: Rhodiola's immunostimulatory activity (T-cell modulation, IFN-gamma, NK cell effects) may oppose the intended effect of immunosuppressants (cyclosporine, tacrolimus, mycophenolate, corticosteroids). Additionally, CYP2C9 inhibition may increase exposure of certain immunosuppressants metabolized by that pathway.
drafts._source_action: Do not use concurrently with immunosuppressant therapy without specialist guidance. Risk of transplant rejection or autoimmune flare if immunosuppression is counteracted.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Rhodiola has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding rhodiola.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Rhodiola affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

drafts._source_action: Monitor immunosuppressant trough levels (tacrolimus, cyclosporine) when starting or stopping silymarin. Dose adjustment by transplant team may be required.

drafts._source_action: Standard vitamin D supplementation is appropriate alongside immunosuppressants. Monitor 25-OH vitamin D levels annually. High-dose supplementation (>4000 IU/day) requires physician oversight.

fields_to_author.informational_note: Vitamin D affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

drafts._source_action: Monitor blood glucose closely when starting or stopping black seed oil in patients on diabetes medications. Warn of additive hypoglycemia risk.

drafts._source_action: Additive blood sugar lowering with diabetes medications. Separate dosing from oral medications by 2 hours (fiber impairs drug absorption). Monitor glucose.

drafts._source_action: Monitor blood glucose when using stinging nettle with diabetes medications. Risk of additive hypoglycemia, particularly with insulin and sulfonylureas.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Stinging nettle can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding stinging nettle and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.

drafts._source_action: Monitor glucose levels when combining with diabetes medications.

drafts._source_action: Use with glucose monitoring if on diabetes treatment.

drafts._source_mechanism: Viscous soluble fiber delays gastric emptying and slows glucose absorption. Meta-analysis of 6 RCTs showed significant reductions in FBG (-1.08 mmol/L) and 2h postprandial glucose.
drafts._source_action: Separate from oral diabetes medications by at least 2 hours (fiber delays drug absorption). Take with adequate water.

drafts._source_mechanism: Soluble viscous fiber delays gastric emptying and slows carbohydrate absorption, reducing postprandial glucose spikes. RCT showed 11% reduction in all-day postprandial glucose.
drafts._source_action: Separate dosing from oral diabetes medications by at least 2 hours (fiber delays drug absorption). Monitor glucose.

drafts._source_mechanism: Tribulus terrestris demonstrated significant blood glucose-lowering effects in a randomized controlled trial of diabetic women (1000 mg/day vs placebo, 3 months; p<0.05 for fasting glucose, 2-hr postprandial glucose, and HbA1c). Additive effect with hypoglycemic medications risks hypoglycemia.
drafts._source_action: Monitor blood glucose when combining with insulin or oral hypoglycemic agents. Dose adjustment of diabetes medications may be required. Caution in patients prone to hypoglycemia.

drafts._source_mechanism: Pharmacologic-dose niacin (≥1g/day) raises HDL-C and lowers triglycerides and LDL-C, but large RCTs (AIM-HIGH, HPS2-THRIVE) failed to show cardiovascular benefit when added to statin therapy, and HPS2-THRIVE found excess serious adverse events with niacin. Extended-release niacin combined with laropiprant was withdrawn in Europe. At doses ≥1g/day, niacin also impairs glucose tolerance.
drafts._source_action: High-dose niacin for cholesterol management has fallen out of favor due to lack of incremental cardiovascular benefit on top of statin therapy and significant side effects. Consult prescriber before initiating pharmacologic niacin for lipid management.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Niacin can affect lipid metabolism. If you are managing high cholesterol, talk to your clinician before adding Niacin — it may change your lipid-therapy plan.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Niacin affects lipid pathways — relevant to people managing high cholesterol.

drafts._source_mechanism: Statins inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, blocking not only cholesterol synthesis but also the mevalonate pathway used to synthesize CoQ10. Plasma and muscle CoQ10 levels are measurably reduced by statin therapy. CoQ10 depletion is proposed as a contributing mechanism to statin-induced myopathy.
drafts._source_action: CoQ10 supplementation (100–300 mg/day) is commonly used to address statin-induced muscle symptoms. It is considered safe to combine. Discuss with prescriber whether supplementation is warranted based on symptoms.

fields_to_author.alert_body: CoQ10 can affect lipid metabolism. If you are managing high cholesterol, talk to your clinician before adding CoQ10 — it may change your lipid-therapy plan.
fields_to_author.informational_note: CoQ10 affects lipid pathways — relevant to people managing high cholesterol.

drafts._source_mechanism: Allicin and other garlic organosulfur compounds inhibit HMG-CoA reductase and cholesterol synthesis. Meta-analyses of RCTs demonstrate modest but significant reductions in total cholesterol (approximately 17 mg/dL) and LDL-C. The effect is moderate and not sufficient as monotherapy for significant hypercholesterolemia.
drafts._source_action: Garlic supplements may contribute modest cholesterol-lowering. If used alongside lipid-lowering medication, monitor lipid panel. Not a substitute for prescribed therapy.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Garlic can affect lipid metabolism. If you are managing high cholesterol, talk to your clinician before adding garlic — it may change your lipid-therapy plan.

drafts._source_mechanism: Berberine activates PCSK9 degradation, upregulates LDL receptors, and has demonstrated significant lipid-lowering effects in multiple RCTs, including reductions in total cholesterol, LDL-C, and triglycerides comparable to some statins. This makes berberine a candidate adjunct for hypercholesterolemia management.
drafts._source_action: Berberine may lower LDL cholesterol meaningfully. If combined with statins, monitor lipid panel and be alert to additive effects. Discuss with prescriber.

drafts._source_mechanism: Bergamot polyphenols (neoriocitrin, naringin, neohesperidin) inhibit HMG-CoA reductase activity and reduce lipid accumulation in adipocytes. Human clinical trials have reported meaningful reductions in LDL-C and total cholesterol. The mechanism of action overlaps with statins.
drafts._source_action: May provide additive cholesterol-lowering benefit when used alongside dietary modifications. If combined with statin therapy, monitor lipid levels and be alert to additive LDL-lowering. Dose adjustment of statin may be needed if cholesterol falls excessively low.

drafts._source_mechanism: Berberine inhibits CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, which are primary metabolic and transport pathways for many statins (simvastatin, atorvastatin, lovastatin). Inhibition of CYP3A4 can increase plasma statin concentrations, potentially raising myopathy and rhabdomyolysis risk. Additive LDL-lowering also occurs.
drafts._source_action: Avoid combining berberine with high-dose CYP3A4-metabolized statins without prescriber oversight. If combined, start with lower statin doses and monitor for muscle symptoms (myalgia, weakness, dark urine).

fields_to_author.alert_body: Berberine shares muscle or liver pathways with statins. If you take a statin, talk to your prescriber before adding berberine and watch for new muscle aches.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Berberine affects statin pathways — relevant to people on statin therapy.

drafts._source_mechanism: High-dose niacin combined with statins increases the risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis beyond that of statin monotherapy. This risk is particularly elevated with gemfibrozil-statin combinations, but niacin-statin combinations also carry increased myopathy risk, estimated at approximately 0.12% incidence in combination therapy trials. The mechanism involves pharmacodynamic additive muscle toxicity.
drafts._source_action: If high-dose niacin (≥1g/day) is co-prescribed with statins, monitor for myopathy symptoms (muscle pain, weakness, dark urine). Obtain baseline and periodic CK levels. Avoid high-intensity statin doses in this combination.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Niacin shares muscle or liver pathways with statins. If you take a statin, talk to your prescriber before adding Niacin and watch for new muscle aches.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Niacin affects statin pathways — relevant to people on statin therapy.

drafts._source_mechanism: Silymarin inhibits CYP3A4 and OATP1B1 transport, which are primary pathways for simvastatin, lovastatin, and atorvastatin metabolism. Elevated statin plasma levels increase myopathy and rhabdomyolysis risk.
drafts._source_action: Use the lowest effective statin dose when combining with silymarin. Monitor for myalgia, weakness, or elevated CK. Avoid high-dose simvastatin or lovastatin.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Silymarin shares muscle or liver pathways with statins. If you take a statin, talk to your prescriber before adding silymarin and watch for new muscle aches.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Silymarin affects statin pathways — relevant to people on statin therapy.

drafts._source_mechanism: Statins deplete endogenous CoQ10 by blocking the mevalonate pathway. CoQ10 supplementation is commonly used to mitigate statin-associated myopathy. No adverse pharmacokinetic interaction between CoQ10 and statins has been demonstrated.
drafts._source_action: CoQ10 100–300 mg/day may be used alongside statins, particularly if myopathy symptoms are present. Safe to co-administer.

fields_to_author.alert_body: CoQ10 shares muscle or liver pathways with statins. If you take a statin, talk to your prescriber before adding CoQ10 and watch for new muscle aches.
fields_to_author.informational_note: CoQ10 affects statin pathways — relevant to people on statin therapy.

drafts._source_mechanism: Garlic may weakly inhibit CYP3A4 and CYP2C9, potentially increasing plasma levels of some statins metabolized by these enzymes. Additionally, additive LDL-lowering effects may occur. The clinical magnitude of the pharmacokinetic interaction is considered modest but relevant at high garlic extract doses.
drafts._source_action: Monitor lipid levels when combining garlic supplements with statin therapy. Report any muscle symptoms.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Garlic shares muscle or liver pathways with statins. If you take a statin, talk to your prescriber before adding garlic and watch for new muscle aches.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Garlic affects statin pathways — relevant to people on statin therapy.

drafts._source_mechanism: Additive HMG-CoA reductase inhibition may produce greater-than-expected LDL reductions when bergamot is combined with statin therapy. Additionally, bergamot contains furanocoumarins that may inhibit CYP3A4, potentially increasing plasma levels of CYP3A4-metabolized statins (simvastatin, atorvastatin, lovastatin).
drafts._source_action: Monitor lipid levels more frequently when initiating bergamot alongside statin therapy. Report any muscle aches or weakness.

fields_to_author.informational_note: Citrus bergamot affects statin pathways — relevant to people on statin therapy.

drafts._source_mechanism: Glycyrrhizin-induced sodium and water retention can worsen fluid overload in patients with impaired renal function, potentially accelerating decline in glomerular filtration rate and exacerbating edema. Patients with CKD are also at greater risk for hypokalemia-related complications given baseline electrolyte dysregulation.
drafts._source_action: Avoid licorice root supplements in chronic kidney disease. If use is unavoidable, monitor serum electrolytes (potassium, sodium) and blood pressure closely.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Licorice root is cleared through the kidneys and may accumulate when kidney function is reduced. If you have kidney disease, talk to your nephrologist about safe intake of licorice root.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Licorice root depends on kidney clearance — relevant to people with kidney conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Propylene glycol is metabolized to lactic acid via alcohol dehydrogenase and ~40-50% is renally excreted. Renal impairment reduces clearance, leading to accumulation and lactic acidosis.
drafts._source_action: Prefer products without propylene glycol when chronic kidney disease is present.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Propylene glycol is cleared through the kidneys and may accumulate when kidney function is reduced. If you have kidney disease, talk to your nephrologist about safe intake of propylene glycol.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Propylene glycol depends on kidney clearance — relevant to people with kidney conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Anthraquinone-induced electrolyte depletion (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia) is amplified in CKD where electrolyte homeostasis is already impaired. Hypokalaemia exacerbates the cardiovascular risk profile in dialysis patients and in those on renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibitors.
drafts._source_action: Avoid prolonged use in kidney disease. If a laxative is necessary, choose an osmotic agent. Monitor electrolytes with any use.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Cascara sagrada is cleared through the kidneys and may accumulate when kidney function is reduced. If you have kidney disease, talk to your nephrologist about safe intake of cascara sagrada.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Cascara sagrada depends on kidney clearance — relevant to people with kidney conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Creatine is metabolised to creatinine and excreted renally. Supplemental creatine reliably elevates serum creatinine, which can spuriously worsen estimated GFR calculations (eGFR via CKD-EPI or MDRD) and complicate monitoring of true kidney function in CKD patients. At high doses, creatine may also increase glomerular filtration pressure.
drafts._source_action: In CKD patients, consult a nephrologist before starting creatine. Clarify with the treating team that serum creatinine elevations may be non-pathological (supplement-related). Avoid creatine loading protocols (>10 g/day) in kidney disease.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Creatine is cleared through the kidneys and may accumulate when kidney function is reduced. If you have kidney disease, talk to your nephrologist about safe intake of creatine.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Creatine depends on kidney clearance — relevant to people with kidney conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Dandelion (Taraxacum officinale) acts as a natural diuretic, increasing urine output via inhibition of renal tubular sodium reabsorption. In CKD, fluid management is critical; uncontrolled diuresis can precipitate dehydration, acute kidney injury, and electrolyte derangements (hypokalemia or hyperkalemia depending on residual kidney function).
drafts._source_action: Avoid unsupervised dandelion supplementation in kidney disease. Any diuretic effect should be managed in consultation with a nephrologist.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Dandelion is cleared through the kidneys and may accumulate when kidney function is reduced. If you have kidney disease, talk to your nephrologist about safe intake of dandelion.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Dandelion depends on kidney clearance — relevant to people with kidney conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Chronic anthraquinone laxative use can cause electrolyte depletion (hypokalemia, hyponatremia) which is poorly tolerated in chronic kidney disease. Potassium wasting compounds the risk of dangerous hyperkalemia rebound and cardiac arrhythmia in CKD patients on renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system blockers.
drafts._source_action: Use senna only short-term in kidney disease. Monitor serum electrolytes with any repeated use; avoid long-term use without nephrologist oversight.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Senna is cleared through the kidneys and may accumulate when kidney function is reduced. If you have kidney disease, talk to your nephrologist about safe intake of senna.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Senna depends on kidney clearance — relevant to people with kidney conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Tribulus terrestris-induced severe nephrotoxicity was documented in a clinical case report (markedly elevated creatinine, resolved on discontinuation). Patients with pre-existing renal impairment are at increased risk. Traditional use as an antiurolithiatic agent paradoxically carries hepato-renal toxicity risk at higher doses.
drafts._source_action: Avoid in patients with chronic kidney disease or impaired renal function. If used in otherwise healthy individuals, limit duration and monitor serum creatinine.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Tribulus is cleared through the kidneys and may accumulate when kidney function is reduced. If you have kidney disease, talk to your nephrologist about safe intake of tribulus.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Tribulus depends on kidney clearance — relevant to people with kidney conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Schisandra chinensis lignans (schisandrin, gomisin A/B/C/N, schisantherin A, schisandrol B) are potent inhibitors of CYP3A4, CYP2C8, CYP2C9, and CYP2C19 in human liver microsomes. Schisandrol B and schisantherin A strongly inhibit CYP3A4 (reversible and mechanism-based inhibition). In a clinical PBPK study, Schisandra sphenanthera co-administration increased tacrolimus AUC by 2.4-fold and midazolam AUC by 2.1-fold. Patients with liver disease on CYP3A4-metabolized drugs (calcineurin inhibitors, statins, antiretrovirals) face clinically significant interaction risk.
drafts._source_action: Avoid in patients with liver disease who are taking CYP3A4-metabolized drugs with narrow therapeutic indices (tacrolimus, cyclosporine, warfarin, many statins, antiretrovirals). If use is unavoidable, TDM of co-administered drugs is mandatory.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Schisandra is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of schisandra.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Schisandra depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Scutellaria species have been linked to herb-induced liver injury (HILI) in published case series. Proposed mechanisms include flavone-mediated mitochondrial dysfunction and idiosyncratic immune-mediated hepatocyte damage. Chinese skullcap preparations adulterated with Teucrium species carry additional germander-related hepatotoxicity risk.
drafts._source_action: Avoid in active liver disease. If use is considered, verify product is authenticated Scutellaria baicalensis (not Teucrium), monitor LFTs monthly, and discontinue immediately upon any elevation.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Chinese skullcap is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of chinese skullcap.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Chinese skullcap depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Copper homeostasis depends on hepatic biliary excretion as the primary elimination route. In Wilson's disease, ATP7B mutations block copper export into bile, causing pathological hepatic accumulation leading to cirrhosis. In non-Wilson's liver disease, impaired biliary function reduces copper clearance, and supplemental copper can accumulate to hepatotoxic levels even at low doses (>2 mg/day).
drafts._source_action: Avoid copper supplementation above 2 mg/day in liver disease. In Wilson's disease, supplemental copper is contraindicated. Consult hepatologist before copper supplementation in chronic liver disease.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Copper is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of copper.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Copper depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: The liver is the principal organ of iron storage and regulation. In liver disease, hepatic hepcidin production is impaired, disrupting iron homeostasis and increasing intestinal iron absorption. Iron supplementation in this context risks exacerbating hepatic iron overload, which drives oxidative stress via the Fenton reaction (Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + OH⁻ + •OH), lipid peroxidation, and progressive liver fibrosis. This mechanism is most critical in hereditary hemochromatosis and alcoholic liver disease, where iron accumulation is already elevated, but applies broadly to chronic liver disease including NAFLD/NASH, hepatitis C, and cirrhosis.
drafts._source_action: Avoid routine iron supplementation in liver disease patients unless iron deficiency anemia is confirmed by labs (serum iron, ferritin, TIBC). Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration. Monitor ferritin and transferrin saturation regularly.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Iron is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of iron.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Iron depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Manganese is excreted almost exclusively via bile. Liver disease impairs biliary excretion, leading to systemic accumulation. Excess manganese deposits in the basal ganglia (globus pallidus), producing manganism — a Parkinson-like neurotoxic syndrome with rigidity, bradykinesia, and psychiatric symptoms. MRI T1 hyperintensity in liver disease patients with elevated manganese is well-documented.
drafts._source_action: Avoid manganese supplementation above 5 mg/day in liver disease. In advanced cirrhosis or liver failure, avoid supplemental manganese entirely. Evaluate manganese status if neurological symptoms develop.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Manganese is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of manganese.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Manganese depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Niacin at doses exceeding 2 g/day causes dose-dependent hepatotoxicity via impairment of mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation and calcium homeostasis disruption. Sustained-release formulations are associated with higher hepatotoxicity risk than immediate-release at equivalent doses. Case reports include acute fulminant hepatic failure requiring ICU admission. In patients with pre-existing liver disease, baseline impaired hepatic metabolism reduces the threshold for injury, and niacin-induced vasodilatation may also exacerbate portal hypertension.
drafts._source_action: Use with caution in liver disease; doses above 500 mg/day should require physician supervision and baseline LFTs. Avoid sustained-release niacin formulations in patients with liver disease. Discontinue if ALT/AST elevations exceed 3x ULN.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Niacin is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of Niacin.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Niacin depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Silymarin (milk thistle extract) has hepatoprotective effects via antioxidant activity and stimulation of ribosomal RNA polymerase I. However, silymarin is a potent inhibitor of CYP3A4, CYP2C9, UGT1A1, and P-glycoprotein. In patients with liver disease on polypharmacy, this CYP inhibition can increase plasma concentrations of co-administered hepatically metabolised drugs, paradoxically worsening hepatotoxic drug burden.
drafts._source_action: In liver disease patients on multiple medications, review all concurrent drugs for CYP3A4/2C9 interactions before starting silymarin. Clinically meaningful interactions exist with immunosuppressants (tacrolimus, cyclosporine), statins, and antivirals. Benefit-risk assessment required.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Silymarin is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of silymarin.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Silymarin depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Retinol (preformed vitamin A) is stored in hepatic stellate cells (Ito cells) as retinyl esters. Chronic liver disease impairs retinol clearance and stellate cell function, causing accumulation even at doses below standard UL. Hypervitaminosis A induces stellate cell hypertrophy and proliferation, leading to progressive fibrosis, non-cirrhotic portal hypertension, and eventual cirrhosis. CYP2E1 induction (common in alcoholic liver disease) enhances retinol hepatotoxicity via microsomal oxidative activation. Clinically relevant injury has been reported at supplemental doses ≥25,000 IU/day in individuals with underlying liver disease.
drafts._source_action: Limit supplemental preformed vitamin A (retinol/retinyl esters) to ≤5,000 IU/day in patients with liver disease. Beta-carotene is preferred. Monitor LFTs and clinical signs of hypervitaminosis (hepatomegaly, ascites). Avoid multivitamins with high retinol doses.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Vitamin A is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of Vitamin A.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Vitamin A depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Despite hepatoprotective activity documented in some animal models, clinical case reports and DILI network databases document rare but real cases of drug-induced liver injury (DILI) associated with Andrographis paniculata supplementation, presenting as cholestatic or mixed hepatocellular injury. Patients with pre-existing liver disease have reduced hepatic reserve and may be at amplified risk.
drafts._source_action: Avoid Andrographis supplementation in patients with active liver disease or significantly elevated transaminases. If used, monitor liver enzymes (ALT, AST) at baseline and after 4-8 weeks of use. Discontinue immediately if hepatic symptoms develop.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Andrographis is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of andrographis.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Andrographis depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Centella asiatica triterpenoids (asiaticoside, madecassoside) have been associated with idiosyncratic hepatotoxicity in case reports. Three cases of jaundice with markedly elevated ALT (324–1694 U/L) and ALP were reported after 20–60 days of use; one patient had recurrence on rechallenge. Immune-mediated mechanisms (autoantibodies, granulomatous histology) are hypothesized. Risk is heightened in patients with pre-existing liver disease.
drafts._source_action: Avoid gotu kola in patients with active hepatic disease or elevated liver enzymes. If used, monitor LFTs at baseline and after 4–6 weeks. Discontinue immediately if transaminases rise.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Gotu kola is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of gotu kola.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Gotu kola depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Silymarin (the active flavonolignan complex from Silybum marianum) is hepatoprotective and widely used in liver disease patients — it reduces oxidative stress, inhibits inflammatory signaling, and has antifibrotic properties. However, silymarin and its constituents (especially silybin A, isosilybin A/B, silychristin) are mechanism-based inhibitors of CYP3A4 and also inhibit P-glycoprotein. In liver disease patients who are on hepatically-cleared medications (antivirals, immunosuppressants, anticoagulants), this CYP3A4 inhibition can elevate drug plasma levels to toxic ranges. The herb itself does not cause hepatotoxicity at standard doses.
drafts._source_action: Milk thistle is generally beneficial in liver disease but requires monitoring for drug interactions in patients on CYP3A4-metabolized medications. Review the full medication list before initiating. Preferred formulation: standardized silymarin 70–80%, 140–420 mg/day.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Milk thistle is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of milk thistle.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Milk thistle depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Saw palmetto (Serenoa repens) has been associated with rare cholestatic hepatitis and, in at least one case, simultaneous acute hepatitis and pancreatitis with rechallenge confirmation. The mechanism is likely idiosyncratic and related to lipophilic fatty acid esters in the extract undergoing hepatic esterase hydrolysis. Standard rat studies at 2–5x human doses showed no liver toxicity markers, suggesting the risk is low but not zero. Pre-existing liver disease may reduce the threshold for idiosyncratic reactions.
drafts._source_action: Use with caution in liver disease. Monitor baseline and periodic LFTs (ALT, ALP, bilirubin) if saw palmetto is initiated in patients with liver disease. Discontinue at first sign of cholestasis (jaundice, elevated ALP, pruritus).

fields_to_author.alert_body: Saw palmetto is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of saw palmetto.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Saw palmetto depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: A case report documented Tribulus terrestris-induced hepatotoxicity alongside nephrotoxicity and neurotoxicity in a healthy male after 2 days of use (ALT elevated, resolved on discontinuation). Pre-existing liver disease may increase vulnerability to hepatotoxic insult.
drafts._source_action: Avoid in patients with active hepatic disease. If used, monitor liver function tests. Discontinue at the first sign of hepatic adverse effects.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Tribulus is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of tribulus.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Tribulus depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Dietary turmeric and culinary curcumin use is generally safe and even hepatoprotective at low doses. However, high-dose concentrated curcumin supplements — particularly formulations co-administered with piperine (black pepper extract), which can increase curcumin bioavailability up to 20-fold — have been implicated in drug-induced liver injury (DILI) case reports with cholestatic and hepatocellular patterns. The proposed mechanism involves CYP1A2 and CYP3A4 inhibition by curcumin, leading to reactive metabolite accumulation. Patients with existing liver disease may have impaired detoxification capacity, lowering the safety threshold.
drafts._source_action: Culinary turmeric is acceptable. Avoid high-dose curcumin supplement formulations (>500 mg curcumin/day) especially those combined with piperine in patients with liver disease. Monitor LFTs if high-dose curcumin is initiated.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Turmeric is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of turmeric.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Turmeric depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Valerian root (Valeriana officinalis) is associated with rare idiosyncratic hepatotoxicity based on published case reports. The mechanism is not fully established; isovaleric acid and valerenic acid undergo hepatic oxidation via CYP2C9, and valerian also contains small amounts of hepatotoxic alkaloids. The overall incidence of liver injury is low, but patients with pre-existing liver disease have reduced hepatic reserve and may be at higher risk from idiosyncratic reactions.
drafts._source_action: Monitor liver function (ALT, AST) if valerian is used long-term in patients with liver disease. Discontinue immediately if transaminase elevations are detected.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Valerian is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of valerian.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Valerian depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drafts._source_mechanism: Salvia officinalis (sage) is traditionally used to suppress lactation (antilactogenic effect). The mechanism involves thujone, camphor, and other volatile terpenes which may have estrogenic and/or dopaminergic effects that reduce prolactin and milk production. Sage tea is used as a traditional remedy to wean nursing infants or reduce painful engorgement. Conversely, nursing mothers who consume sage supplements may experience unwanted milk reduction. Thujone in high concentrations can also be neurotoxic.
drafts._source_action: Avoid sage supplements during established breastfeeding if continuing lactation is desired, as they may reduce milk supply. Culinary amounts of sage in food are generally safe. High-dose sage extracts should be avoided.

shortcut_from_existing.alert_headline: Concentrated sage may affect milk supply
shortcut_from_existing.alert_body: Sage at supplement or concentrated doses may reduce milk supply. If you are breastfeeding, talk to your clinician before using concentrated forms; culinary use at typical amounts is fine.
shortcut_from_existing.informational_note: Concentrated sage can affect lactation — culinary amounts are appropriate for anyone breastfeeding.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Sage at supplement or concentrated doses may reduce milk supply. If you are breastfeeding, talk to your clinician before using concentrated forms; culinary use at typical amounts is fine.

drafts._source_mechanism: High-dose pyridoxine (vitamin B6) has been investigated as a pharmacologic agent to suppress postpartum lactation. At doses of 450–600 mg/day for 5–7 days, two trials found ~95% inhibition of lactation, though four other studies failed to demonstrate efficacy by prolactin measurement or clinical assessment. The proposed mechanism is dopaminergic: pyridoxine is a cofactor in dopamine synthesis; elevated dopamine tonically inhibits prolactin release from the anterior pituitary. A 1979 NEJM case report warned of dangers of vitamin B6 in nursing mothers at high doses. Normal multivitamin doses (1.9 mg/day recommended for lactation) are not a concern.
drafts._source_action: Standard prenatal vitamin doses of B6 are safe during lactation. High-dose B6 supplementation (>50 mg/day) during established breastfeeding may suppress milk production. Avoid high-dose B6 if breastfeeding is intended. Consult lactation specialist before use.

shortcut_from_existing.alert_headline: High-dose B6 may affect milk supply
shortcut_from_existing.alert_body: Pharmacologic B6 doses may reduce milk supply via prolactin suppression. If you are breastfeeding, talk to your clinician before using concentrated B6; prenatal-vitamin levels of B6 are appropriate.
shortcut_from_existing.informational_note: High-dose B6 can affect lactation — prenatal-dose B6 is appropriate for anyone breastfeeding.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Pharmacologic B6 doses may reduce milk supply via prolactin suppression. If you are breastfeeding, talk to your clinician before using concentrated B6; prenatal-vitamin levels of B6 are appropriate.
fields_to_author.informational_note: High-dose B6 can affect lactation — prenatal-dose B6 is appropriate for anyone breastfeeding.

drafts._source_mechanism: Fenugreek is one of the most widely used galactagogues globally. Proposed mechanism involves stimulation of sweat glands (structurally similar to mammary glands) via phytoestrogens, and possibly dopaminergic pathways affecting prolactin. While some women report increased milk volume, a 2022 Australian cross-sectional survey found widespread use with incomplete evidence of efficacy. Fenugreek's volatile compounds (diosgenin, 4-hydroxyisoleucine) are excreted in breast milk and may cause GI upset (colic, diarrhea, unusual maple-syrup body odor) in the nursing infant.
drafts._source_action: Fenugreek is widely used as a galactagogue but evidence for efficacy is inconsistent. If used, monitor nursing infant for GI symptoms (excessive gas, diarrhea, colic). Do not use in infants with maple syrup urine disease family history. Avoid doses >3g/day without lactation consultant guidance.

shortcut_from_existing.alert_body: Fenugreek has uterotonic activity and can lower maternal blood sugar. If you are pregnant, talk to your obstetrician; during breastfeeding, use under clinician guidance for milk-supply support.
shortcut_from_existing.informational_note: Fenugreek has uterotonic activity — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

drafts._source_action: Avoid routine use during pregnancy unless clinician-directed.

drafts._source_mechanism: Sennosides stimulate myenteric plexus nerve endings and increase colonic peristalsis via prostaglandin release. Chronic or high-dose use may trigger uterine smooth muscle contractions; limited human data show occasional association with preterm labor at high doses.
drafts._source_action: Avoid prolonged or high-dose use during pregnancy. Osmotic laxatives are preferred. Consult an obstetrician for any laxative use beyond occasional short-term relief.

drafts._source_action: Avoid high-dose preformed vitamin A in pregnancy unless clinician-directed.

drafts._source_mechanism: Chasteberry has been studied for luteal phase defect and hyperprolactinemia-related infertility, showing possible benefit in some trials. However, it alters LH pulsatility and progesterone levels, which could be counterproductive if the underlying cause of infertility differs. Timing discontinuation at confirmed conception is critical as the hormonal window for implantation is sensitive.
drafts._source_action: If using chasteberry while trying to conceive, plan to discontinue immediately upon positive pregnancy test. Use should be under the guidance of a fertility specialist.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Chasteberry used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of chasteberry with your clinician.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Chasteberry is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

drafts._source_mechanism: Saw palmetto (Serenoa repens) inhibits 5-alpha-reductase (both type 1 and type 2), reducing conversion of testosterone to the more potent dihydrotestosterone (DHT). In males, this mechanism is the basis for its use in benign prostatic hyperplasia and androgenic alopecia. However, 5-alpha-reductase inhibition in men who are TTC can impair semen quality, reduce seminal fluid volume, and potentially reduce sperm motility. Additionally, saw palmetto may have anti-androgenic effects that interfere with male reproductive hormone signaling needed for spermatogenesis.
drafts._source_action: Men who are actively trying to conceive should discontinue saw palmetto supplementation. 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (including saw palmetto) may adversely affect semen parameters and male fertility. Discuss with reproductive urologist if fertility is a concern.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Saw palmetto used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of saw palmetto with your clinician.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Saw palmetto is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

drafts._source_mechanism: Exogenous phytoestrogenic compounds during the preconception period may disrupt the natural hormonal cycle regulation required for ovulation, implantation, and early embryo development. Diosgenin's estrogenic receptor activity is documented in animal models; human TTC data are absent.
drafts._source_action: Caution is warranted when trying to conceive. Consult a reproductive endocrinologist or OB-GYN before using wild yam supplements periconceptionally.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Wild yam used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of wild yam with your clinician.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Wild yam is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

drafts._source_mechanism: CoQ10 is an essential mitochondrial antioxidant. Oocyte quality declines with age partly due to reduced mitochondrial CoQ10 availability and impaired ATP production. Ben-Meir et al. (2015, Aging Cell) demonstrated that CoQ10 supplementation reversed age-related oocyte deficits in mice, prevented premature ovarian failure in Pdss2-deficient animals, and improved meiotic spindle integrity. A 2024 meta-analysis of 20 RCTs (Shang et al.) found CoQ10 improved retrieved oocyte count, high-quality embryo rates, and clinical pregnancy rates in women with ovarian aging, with optimal effect at 30 mg/day for 3 months before ovarian stimulation in women under 35 with diminished ovarian reserve.
drafts._source_action: CoQ10 supplementation (100–600 mg/day, commonly 200–400 mg/day) may support oocyte quality during the preconception period, especially in women over 35 or those with diminished ovarian reserve. Safe to use during TTC. Discontinue when pregnancy is confirmed to return to standard prenatal supplement regimen.

fields_to_author.alert_body: CoQ10 used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of CoQ10 with your clinician.
fields_to_author.informational_note: CoQ10 is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

drafts._source_mechanism: Periconceptional folic acid/folate supplementation reduces neural tube defect (NTD) risk by 69% (RR 0.31, 95% CI 0.17–0.58) in Cochrane meta-analysis of 5 RCTs (6708 births). The protective effect operates during neural tube closure (days 21–28 post-conception), before many women know they are pregnant. Supplementation should therefore begin at least 1 month (ideally 3 months) before conception. For women with MTHFR polymorphisms, methylfolate (5-MTHF) is preferred as it bypasses the enzymatic conversion step.
drafts._source_action: Begin folate/folic acid supplementation at least 1 month before attempting conception. Standard dose: 400–800 mcg/day for low-risk women; 4 mg/day (4000 mcg) for women with prior NTD-affected pregnancy. Prefer methylfolate (5-MTHF) if MTHFR variant is known. Continue through first trimester.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Folate used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of Folate with your clinician.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Folate is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

drafts._source_mechanism: Myo-inositol (the primary isomer in supplements) is a second messenger in insulin signaling and plays a critical role in ovarian follicle maturation. In PCOS, insulin resistance disrupts the inositol phosphoglycan mediator system. Supplementation at 2–4 g/day myo-inositol (alone or in 40:1 ratio with D-chiro-inositol) improves ovulation rates, menstrual regularity, and fertility outcomes. An RCT (Agrawal et al. 2019) found significantly higher live birth rates with metformin + myo-inositol (55%) vs. metformin alone (26.67%, p=0.002) in PCOS women undergoing ovulation induction.
drafts._source_action: Myo-inositol (2–4 g/day) is a well-supported supplement for women with PCOS who are TTC. It improves ovulation and insulin sensitivity. Safe for TTC use. Also safe to continue in early pregnancy under clinician guidance. Not specifically indicated for non-PCOS TTC without ovulation issues.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Inositol used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of inositol with your clinician.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Inositol is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

drafts._source_mechanism: Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) is essential for DNA synthesis, cell division, and one-carbon metabolism. B12 deficiency elevates homocysteine via impaired methionine synthase activity, and hyperhomocysteinemia is associated with recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL), implantation failure, and embryotoxicity. Case-control studies document significantly lower serum B12 in women with recurrent miscarriage vs. controls (mean 197 vs 300 pg/mL, p=0.004 in one Syrian cohort; 178 vs 269 pg/mL, p<0.001 in a Polish cohort). B12 is a standard component of preconception care and prenatal vitamins. Optimizing B12 status before conception is associated with improved fertility outcomes and reduced RPL risk via homocysteine normalization.
drafts._source_action: Ensure adequate vitamin B12 status (serum B12 >400 pg/mL is optimal preconceptionally) when trying to conceive. Standard preconception supplementation: 2.4–6 mcg/day (adequate intake) up to higher doses if deficient. B12 supplementation is recommended as part of a comprehensive preconception vitamin regimen for all women TTC, and is particularly important for vegans, vegetarians, those with gastric conditions, or those on metformin (which reduces B12 absorption).

fields_to_author.alert_body: Vitamin B12 used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of Vitamin B12 with your clinician.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Vitamin B12 is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

drafts._source_mechanism: Vitamin D receptors (VDR) are expressed in ovarian granulosa cells, endometrium, and placenta. A 2017 review (Voulgaris et al., Hormones) summarized strong associations between vitamin D status and PCOS metabolic parameters, endometriosis, and IVF outcomes, with deficiency linked to lower clinical pregnancy rates. Meta-analyses of IVF studies suggest women with 25(OH)D >20 ng/mL have significantly better outcomes, though causality is not fully established.
drafts._source_action: Check 25(OH)D levels prior to or during TTC. Correct deficiency (<20 ng/mL) with supplementation (1000–2000 IU/day) under clinician guidance. Achieving sufficiency (30–60 ng/mL) is recommended before and during early pregnancy. Standard prenatal vitamins provide ~400–1000 IU/day.

fields_to_author.alert_body: Vitamin D used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of Vitamin D with your clinician.
fields_to_author.informational_note: Vitamin D is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

drafts._source_mechanism: DHEA (dehydroepiandrosterone) supplementation at 75 mg/day for ≥12 weeks has been studied in women with diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) undergoing IVF. The DITTO RCT protocol targeted women with AFC ≤10 and AMH ≤5 pmol/L. A controlled case-series (Barad & Gleicher 2006) showed significant improvements in fertilized oocytes, normal Day-3 embryo count, and embryos transferred after DHEA treatment. Proposed mechanism: DHEA converts to androgens and estrogens in follicular cells, improving follicular microenvironment and reducing follicle apoptosis. DHEA should not be used without hormonal workup confirming DOR, as it may disrupt normal hormonal balance in women without ovarian reserve issues.
drafts._source_action: DHEA at 75 mg/day may be considered for women with confirmed diminished ovarian reserve, ideally under reproductive endocrinologist guidance. Monitor DHEA-S and androgen levels. Not appropriate for general TTC use without confirmed DOR diagnosis.

fields_to_author.alert_body: DHEA used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of DHEA with your clinician.
fields_to_author.informational_note: DHEA is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.
